data.title: Capital One's Impressive Streak
data.description:
“The profit margin for credit card companies now exceeds 50%. One of the biggest. And they charge Americans interest rates of 28%, 30%, 31%, 32%. What happened to usury?” – Donald Trump

The weekend before last, President Trump made some critical comments about the credit card companies, and he pushed for a 10% cap on the interest rates they’re allowed to charge. After sleeping on those headlines for the rest of that weekend, investors headed into the week looking to unload.

As one example, Capital One (COF) opened sharply lower on that Monday and remains near those levels now. This morning, the president reiterated his critical comments towards the card companies in Davos and called on Congress to do something about it (since he can't actually unilaterally cap rates himself).

For COF specifically, the company will report earnings on Thursday after the close, and investors will be looking for any comments from company management regarding how they plan to react to the president’s animosity towards them and any legislative push that arises from it.

We’re not sure exactly what (besides the mid-terms) made the president so focused on the credit card companies lately, but maybe it’s the performance of COF in reaction to its earnings reports over the last three years.

The snapshot below comes from our Earnings Explorer and shows COF’s earnings results and subsequent stock performance in reaction to each of those reports.

Since the bull market began in October 2022, COF has exceeded EPS forecasts only 7 out of 13 times and beat sales forecasts just 6 times. Combined, the company has exceeded EPS and sales forecasts in the same earnings report only three times. Based on its results relative to expectations, it doesn’t appear as though COF has been minting money.

While COF’s earnings results haven’t been particularly strong, the stock’s reaction to those earnings reports has been stellar. As shown on the right side of the table, since Q4 2022, the stock’s earnings reaction day performance has been positive all 13 times for an average one-day gain of 3.5. Shares have seen one-day gains in response to earnings on each of its last 13 reports!

Besides the positive earnings reaction day performance, COF has been a very strong performer between earnings reports. Since the bull market began in October 2022, the stock has rallied 156%, which is 66 percentage points more than the S&P 500.

It may not be the best performer in the S&P 500 during this bull market, but COF has been one of the better performers. That's great if you're a shareholder, but it also makes for an easy scapegoat when an issue like affordability is poised to be a central theme of this year's mid-term elections. What other issue can you think of out there where Donald Trump and Elizabeth Warren would be on the same page?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fujifilm: Diversified Exposure To AI Through Chips, Software, And Healthcare
data.description:
Obtaining AI exposure through chips has become expensive with the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) trading above 24x earnings amid bubble talk. Also, despite their enterprise potential, software stocks have suffered a sell-off.

In these conditions, while highlighting the risks, this thesis aims to highlight the relevance of the diversified exposure available through Japan’s FUJIFILM (FUJIY), which trades at a significant discount relative to IT peers, as shown below.

Its advantages are that, in addition to producing the materials used to build chips critical for rapidly moving data in data centers, it also provides Generative AI solutions to optimize business workflows, without forgetting its strong foothold in healthcare, a sector being transformed by innovation.

This implies that it can play a diversifier role in an innovation landscape that is characterized by volatility, and I start by emphasizing its main growth driver, semis materials.

Semiconductor Materials, Innovative Software, and AI-for-Healthcare

First, it is a top producer of CMP (chemical mechanical planarization) slurry, a polishing solution used for creating super-flat wafer surfaces, essential to build high-density chips. Looking specifically at the related AI applications, its copper interconnect CMP slurry is seeing traction for producing ultra-precise connections for super-fast chips, with limited signal loss.

Still, revenue for the Electronics segment (comprising Semiconductor materials) grew by only 4.7% YoY in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 (FQ2-26), which ended in September last year, as illustrated below. Such single-digit growth is due to a decline in the other materials business, namely AF (Advanced Functional), due to lower demand for data tape.

Now, FUJIFILM does not publish revenue figures for its CMP slurry business separately, but it has been mentioned that related sales have been growing at a CAGR of 19% over the 10 years through 2024. Notably, this is more than double the market rate of growth of 8.12%, and with the help of the recently launched (September 2025) advanced packaging slurry aimed at enhancing chip performance (as components become more miniaturized), revenues for the Electronics segment can grow faster.

Second, there is the Business Innovations segment, which made up 36% of total sales in FQ2, growing at a pace of 3.8% YoY. Looking ahead, it can rely on a Gen AI-driven intelligent data capture tool called IWpro, which enables automatic capture and structuring of data from paper-based documents (like invoices). Additionally, its partnership with Singapore-based ReN3 allows it to propose agentic AI solutions, a market expected to grow by 44.6% CAGR from 2025 to 2032.

Third, with its Synapse AI Orchestrator, designed to offer intelligent medical imaging services, the company can tap into its large healthcare customer base (which accounted for nearly 33% of sales in FQ2-2026) and to which it already sells equipment and materials geared at discovering and manufacturing drugs and other pharmaceutical products. Furthermore, embedding AI into existing devices can turn out to be relatively more profitable. The reason is that upselling AI-enhanced products to its existing clients entails higher prices while doing less effort compared to penetrating a new market.

Deserves Better, Even After Adopting Moderation

Therefore, the ingredients are present to drive revenue growth and support higher profitability. Also, in addition to chip materials, FUJIFILM has made progress in healthcare while exhibiting continued strength in the imaging segment, which accounted for 17.8% of FQ2-26 sales, as shown above.

Hence, it deserves a higher value considering it is trading at a significant discount, both for the price-to-sales and price-to-earnings multiples, as shown above. Taking into account the difference between its trailing GAAP P/E of 13.76x and the IT sector median of 33.33x at the time of writing, it can potentially appreciate by roughly 59% ((13.76-33.33)/33.33).

Now, this is significant, but it is important to adopt a cautious tone, as both revenue and EPS for FY-26 are expected to decline compared to last year (FY-25), as summarized in the table below.

Identifying the reasons, first, the company is reducing the sales of low-margin products in China, which should impact the Business Innovation segment (which includes software). Second, its medical devices have been impacted by higher production costs related to raw materials, namely steel, and this may continue because industrial metals have continued to be on a tear since last year. Third, U.S. tariffs imposed on Japanese goods should also eat into margins. Fourth, as touched upon earlier, its AF material has not enjoyed the same level of success as last year because of the absence of big orders from IT companies.

In these circumstances, a more reasonable premium would be 20% or about a third of the 59% discount it is trading at relative to the IT sector. Hence, adjusting its share price of $10.77 at the time of writing accordingly, I obtain a target of roughly $13 (10.77 x 1.2), which, by the way, equates to the average of what analysts forecast. Diving into the price action, the stock went above $15 in September 2021 when FUJIFILM's stock benefited from a recovery in printing, and this after COVID had hurt sales volumes. However, subsequently, the shares slid before recovering with the $13.6-$13.8 constituting a resistance level. Hence, taking into account the AI potential but also adopting moderation because of the risks mentioned below, $13 is reasonable for the medium term.

Highlighting the Risks

First, looking at the broader picture, both the Nasdaq 100 Composite and the tech-heavy Nikkei 225 indices have surged by more than 100% during the last three years, as shown below, largely driven by the AI boom. However, this performance has not been linear, marked by several periods of volatility, one of which occurred after China’s DeepSeek launched an AI model that delivered performances comparable with ChatGPT in January 2025, but at a much lower cost. Now, with China heavily investing in innovation, there are likely to be other DeepSeek moments, likely to impact the Nikkei index.

Second, Japan (a close U.S. ally) and China have been in a significant diplomatic tussle since late 2025, following Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi's remarks around Taiwan. In response, Chinese retaliatory economic measures have targeted seafood imports, travel, and, more recently, rare earth metals, instilling uncertainty in the Japanese supply chain.

Third, it faces competition from Fujimi (OTCPK:FUJXF), which is more specialized in precision abrasives and slurries used for memory chips and hard disks. Now, the high demand for these products explains Fujimi's higher net profit margins.

Still, continuing on an optimistic note, FUJIFILM has a broader semiconductor portfolio, not only selling CMP slurries but also related products like EUV photoresists, behaving more as an integrated supplier of semiconductor materials, in addition to being diversified in imaging, healthcare, and business software. As a result, it has been less volatile, as shown below.

Diversification Strength and Yen Depreciation Justify a Bullish Case

To further justify my bullish position, growth is likely to be more sustainable in the future. The reason is that its CMP slurries, which are critical to the production of interconnect chips, are in high demand and have led to the semiconductor materials becoming a key growth driver for FUJIFILM, as mentioned above. In response, it has carried out massive investments of over 100 billion yen from FY2021 to FY2024 in facilities, resulting in sales surging by about 1.7 times during this period.

Now, since the plan is to accelerate Capex by at least 100 billion yen within two years (from FY2025 to FY2026 compared to four years earlier), sales are expected to grow faster, especially given that the Zwijndrecht (Belgium) facility is scheduled to begin operations in spring 2026. This may be why the company is forecasted to rebound to growth in FY2027 (ending in March next year) at a rate of 5% YoY (from $20.79 billion to $21.88 billion as per the above table), after being expected to suffer a decline this fiscal year. More importantly, analysts expect the EPS to grow even faster (8% YoY), hinting at better margins. This may be the result of enjoying pricing power normally synonymous with capturing peak demand, as it now becomes an important part of the global chip supply chain.

Still, on a cautionary note, since both sales and margins are expected to decline for the current fiscal year, the stock could become volatile, especially if the guidance for FY-27 is downgraded. Moreover, FCF remains negative as capital spent exceeds cash generated from operations, and it also has more debt than cash, meaning more risk-averse investors may prefer to wait on the sidelines. Along the same lines, the stock could be subject to volatility in case geopolitical tensions deteriorate further because 27% of FQ2-26 sales came from Asia (excluding Japan), which comprises the Chinese market.

Still, the debt-to-equity ratio of 26.4% indicates financial stability, aligned with the management's aim to maintain sufficient liquidity and a "sound capital structure". At the same time, in an environment characterized by uncertainty and trade tensions, FUJIFILM's diversification strength, both product and geographical (table below), makes sense.

Finally, yen depreciation is a positive. In this respect, the financial projections for FY-26 assume that the USD to JPY and EUR to JPY exchange rates would be 145 and 162, respectively. However, the yen has depreciated significantly relative to these two currencies as per the chart below.

Therefore, this means that sales made in USD and EUR are converted back into more yen-denominated revenues. At the same time, products priced in the Japanese currency become cheaper for foreign buyers, not only helping to boost exports but also to partially mitigate the effects of 15% U.S. tariffs. Also, while a weaker yen increases the cost of imported raw materials, given that the company generates 65% of its revenues from exports, the net effect on FUJIFILM is positive. The reason is that sales proceeds (from exports) exceeding expenses (from imports) means improved profits.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Predictions Are Hard: A Realistic Look At U.S. P&C Insurers In 2026
data.description:
December is the season for fortune-tellers, investment oracles, and self-proclaimed market prophets. We are in January; the new year has already started, and I am already late.

Let me start with a simple disclaimer: I am a poor fortune-teller. Please do not take my words as gospel.

This is not an exercise in self-sabotage. I simply know myself reasonably well after nearly 37 years of cohabitation, and I can state the following with some confidence: I am a fairly solid P&C (property and casualty for those who are not insurance fetishists) actuary. I do not usually indulge in self-admiration, but a small dose of self-esteem has yet to harm anyone.

Although I am not based in the United States, I have spent years analyzing U.S.-listed insurance companies, with a particular focus on the P&C sector.

Along the way, I have missed more than a few trains - Progressive (PGR), Palomar (PLMR), Trisura (TSU:CA), to name just a few - and I have also made my share of mistakes, sometimes costly ones. Atlas Financial and Maiden Holdings are still intense reminders that insurance cycles do not forgive overconfidence.

So yes, this exercise is challenging, imperfect, and potentially flawed by design. Predicting the future is never easy. Cassandra foresaw the fall of Troy, yet no one believed her. Nostradamus - or Michel de Nostredame, for the purists - left us riddles rather than clarity.

The following quatrain is attributed to Nostradamus and is often cited as a prediction of the death of King Henry II of France.

Le lyon ieune le vieux ſurmontera

En champ bellique par ſingulier duelle

Dans caige d'or les yeux luy creuera

Deux claſſes vne, puis mourir, mors cruelle.

or in English:

The young lion will overcome the older one,

On the field of battle in a single duel;

He will pierce his eyes through a golden cage,

Two wounds made one, then he dies a cruel death.

If this sounds obscure, that is perfectly normal. It is written in 16th-century French, and its meaning only became “clear” after the event, which, in many ways, tells us more about hindsight than about prophecy itself.

Insurance is not about predicting the future. It is about pricing uncertainty - and surviving long enough to be proven right.

Today, my plan is to provide some hints to the SA community. I won't cover the full North American P&C market.

#1: A Tougher Market Where Underwriting Margins Matter Again

Insurers will publish their FY2025 results over the coming weeks. NAIC has already presented the Q3 2025 industry overview:

Third-quarter results were steady, with a better combined ratio, driven by a strong year-over-year loss ratio reduction. By combining the three quarterly reports, the year-to-date combined ratio improved by 3.4 points.

2025 2024
Loss Ratio (YTD) 69.1% 71.9%
Expense Ratio (YTD) 26.3% 26.9%
Combined Ratio (YTD) 95.4% 98.8%

The FY2025 underwriting margins of the P&C sector will certainly be better than in 2024, several insurance carriers benefiting from rate changes and eased inflation.

What about 2026? As explained in my latest article on PGR, outlook surveys indicate that the market will face headwinds in 2026 and 2027, with softer rate changes.

In a recent article, S&P has estimated that underwriting margins will reduce for most top U.S. P&C carriers in 2026, with a 0.4 point deterioration in the combined ratio. After two years of improved underwriting margins, this trend is unlikely to persist uniformly across the sector.

The root causes? A combination of different effects, such as:

  • Softer commercial motor prices;
  • Costlier weather-driven events;
  • And a more competitive personal motor market.

With softer price increases, higher social inflation, underwriting discipline will matter the most. Less disciplined carriers will be disproportionately impacted compared to consistently stronger insurers. For investors, the priority is selecting resilient insurers. The key metric is the underwriting performance, with a 10-year combined ratio below 100%. If safety is the goal, investors can set the bar closer to 95%. To me, top-notch insurers are the expected targets, as the overperforming P&C carriers will be well-equipped to suffer from the elevated inflation and the pricing competition.

#2: Interest Rate, A double-Edged Sword

Past and upcoming Fed rate cuts are viewed as tailwinds for insurers, as they may pressure investment income growth. In a context of pressured underwriting margins, the weak insurers will struggle even more.

Rate cuts can also support book values and equity valuations for well-capitalized insurance carriers. If equity markets perform well in 2026, insurers with large equity exposure could benefit from the rally. Cincinnati Financial (CINF) is a good example, with roughly 40% of its investment portfolio invested in equities.

As usual, rate cuts will create both losers and winners. The most important part is not rate cuts themselves, but how insurers are positioned to absorb them. To me, lower rate will increase pressure on poorly performing insurers that historically relied on investment income to offset weak results.

#3: A Side Note On AI & Automation

AI and automation are expected to gradually reshape claims management and pricing efficiency over time. But, in 2026, they remain incremental advantages rather than true disruptors. The insurance industry is an old-fashioned sector, where innovations take time to translate into tangible results.

Some insurance companies, with a stronger AI narrative, like Lemonade (LMND), may attract investor attention. Still, investors should not confuse AI messages with sustainable pricing power or underwriting performance. In the end, the golden rule remains the underwriting discipline.

#4: Focus On Boring, Resilient Compounders

In a weaker cycle, investors should prioritize insurers with proven over-cycle underwriting discipline, diversified lines of business, and consistently low expense ratios. SA readers expect few names, so please let me meet your expectations:

Chubb (CB), aka “The One”. If you should choose one name based only on underwriting discipline, it would be Chubb. As mentioned already in my latest company's coverage, Chubb is the best-in-class insurance company in the U.S. in terms of underwriting performance. Over the last ten year, the company’s combined ratio averaged 89.8% vs. 97.8% for its peers.

Thanks to its niche business (e.g. agricultural insurance business in North America), its geographical diversification coupled with a presence in retail and commercial segments, Chubb is the insurance carrier you can’t reject. Furthermore, the company has started to increase its footprint in life activities via its Asian subsidiaries, with consecutive acquisitions over the prior years. Lastly, there were rumors about AIG’s (AIG) acquisition to create a super insurance behemoth. The sole area where investors could complain about is the dividend policy. Despite its dividend aristocrat status, Chubb is less generous than other peers, like Travelers (TRV). In 2026, Chubb remains extremely well-positioned for delivering steady underwriting gains. A combined ratio in the range of 88% to 92% remains achievable in a favorable underwriting environment.

Travelers, aka “The Contender”. Although the underwriting discipline is less steady than Chubb’s, Travelers is a cash machine for investors, combining active share repurchases with high single-digit dividend growth. The capital redistribution is supported by the underwriting performance steadiness, caused by several effects: a presence in specialty markets (fidelity and surety business, and management liability coverages), a focus on reducing the expense ratio over the last decade (from 31.7% during the 2010-2016 period to 28.5% in 2024). Thanks to those combined effects, the 10-year combined ratio was 94.6%.

Furthermore, the investment income also fueled the earnings, the dividend distribution, and the share repurchase programs. Even with a jump of the combined ratio in the 97%ish area, Travelers, Inc. will remain steady, benefiting from the higher asset base, driven by the rate changes implemented over the last years. Furthermore, the sale of the Canadian subsidiary will boost the share repurchase programs.

Progressive, aka “The Motor King”. I used to name Progressive as well “GEICO without Warren Buffett.” The underwriting performance of the motor insurance carrier over the last decade is impressive. While Progressive is present in one of the toughest and most challenging markets, the insurer has succeeded in combining premium growth with higher margins. One key to its success was the expense ratio level, which was below 20%.

Some investors may not view Progressive, as a dividend-rewarding company, and that is true. But the company’s core value is not its dividend policy. It's the consistent execution of underwriting discipline.

Final Thoughts

2026 is unlikely to be a repeat of the easy years for U.S. P&C insurers, given the rise of catastrophe losses and higher inflation. Softer pricing, moderating investment income, and higher social inflation will test underwriting discipline again. To me, this is not a market to chase narratives or short-term momentum.

Investors should focus on insurers that have already proven their ability to navigate through weaker cycles rather than those that relied on favorable conditions. In insurance, discipline is rarely exciting, but it remains the most reliable long-term asset to create value.

data.title: Sizing Up Seven Hills Realty Trust
data.description:
Shares of floating rate first mortgage loan REIT Seven Hills Realty Trust (SEVN) are down nearly 30% in 2025, as Fed easing has squeezed its net interest margins, compelling it to lower its distribution. Even though the dividend was lowered from $0.35 per share to $0.28 in 3Q25, it still yields over 12%, as 23% of its portfolio was rated ‘higher risk’ on September 30, 2025 – all office properties. With rate floors kicking in to stem further margin compression, three new mortgages in November 2025, and a capital raise in December, the insider buying on that offering merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Seven Hills Realty Trust is a Newton, Massachusetts real estate investment trust [REIT] focused on originating and investing in floating rate first mortgage loans that range from $15 million to $75 million, secured by middle-market transitional commercial real estate [CRE] properties in the U.S. with values up to $100 million. The REIT is managed by Tremont Realty Capital, a wholly owned subsidiary of alternative asset manager The RMR Group (RMR), which has originated ~$6 billion of mortgages since its inception in 2000. Seven Hills was founded as RMR Asia Pacific Real Estate Fund in 2002 and went public in 2006, raising net proceeds of $33.4 million at $30 per share, after giving effect to a 2-for-3 reverse stock split in 2009. It rebranded to RMR Mortgage Trust in 2010, changed to a first mortgage lender REIT structure in 2020, and merged with Tremont Mortgage Trust in 2021, creating Seven Hills. Shares of SEVN trade around nine bucks a share, equating to an approximate market cap of $200 million.

Approach & Portfolio

With a sizable funnel of 450-500 transitional CRE candidates annually, the REIT issues term sheets on approximately 10% of its screened loans with ~15% of them consummating in a first mortgage, featuring an average size of ~$30 million. Transitional CRE represents properties subject to redevelopment or repositioning activities. The loans are typically two to five years in term, with some including extension options. Management is currently pursuing opportunities in multifamily – particularly student housing – grocery-anchored retail, and hospitality.

On September 30, 2025, Seven Hills’ portfolio consisted of 22 loans totaling $641.9 million with diversification across geography, with 39% of its commitments (nine properties) in the South. The variation also applies to property type with six multifamily, six office, five industrial, three hotels, and one each of mixed use and retail loans comprising its holdings. The weighted average coupon rate on its mortgages was SOFR plus 3.61%, with an all-in yield, which included amortization of deferred fees over the initial term of the loan, at SOFR plus 3.97%. That translated to actual yields of 7.39% and 7.85% (respectively) with the weighted average maximum maturity of 2.6 years.

Weighted average loan-to-value was 67% with a weighted average risk weighting of 2.9 on a scale comprised of: 1- lower risk (2 properties); 2-average risk (3); 3- acceptable risk (12); 4- higher risk (5); and 5- impaired/loss likely (0). Of note is Seven Hills’ office loan portfolio, with five of its six properties rated 4, representing principal of $141 million with an average occupancy rate of 87% and lease term of 4.2 years. Sponsors have contributed $9.8 million on those properties to fund reserves.

To finance its mortgages, the REIT has secured financing facilities from four lenders totaling $740 million with floating interest rates of SOFR plus a spread ranging from 1.75% to 2.95%. The average advance rate (financing as a percentage of collateral) on those loans is ~70%.

Share Price Performance

With a floating rate portfolio in an environment of Fed easing, Seven Hills returns have suffered both on an absolute and net interest margin [NIM] basis. In FY23, its portfolio generated a weighted average coupon rate of $9.00% with a NIM of 1.77%. By contrast, over the first nine months of 2025, the REIT’s portfolio produced a weighted average coupon rate of 7.99% and an NIM of 1.55%. As such, management was compelled to lower its quarterly disbursement from $0.35 a share, which it had maintained for ten consecutive quarters ending 2Q25, to $0.28 a share, presently. Investors are still skeptical on its ability to pay its recently lowered distribution, considering its payout ratio in 3Q25 was 97%. As a result, shares of SEVN have lost over 30% of their value since YE24, carrying a current yield of 12.2%.

However, all but one of its loans has an interest rate floor, and with SOFR hovering around 4%, two of its floors have activated, with another five that are subject to activation down another 25 basis points and still another six down 50 basis points. By contrast, none of its floating rate financing facilities are subject to a floor. That dynamic translates into less pressure on NIM if the Fed continues to ease. Furthermore, with rates coming down and corporate bond spreads tightening, there is a growing demand for floating rate refinancing and (for now) an influx of capital to meet it, rendering the lending environment competitive but laced with opportunity. In total, ~$1.96 trillion of CRE debt will mature in 2026-2029, the largest component of which is multifamily (~$800 billion).

Recent Secondary Offering & New Investments

In this CRE milieu, Seven Hills elected to conduct a fully backstopped transferable rights offering, raising gross proceeds of $65.2 million at $8.65 per share on December 11, 2025 – more on that below. The proceeds are earmarked for portfolio expansion, some of which occurred in November 2025, encompassing three first mortgages for a student housing property in Maryland, a Marriott hotel in Massachusetts, and an industrial property in Pennsylvania. The loans totaled $101.3 million.

3Q25 Financial Report & 4Q25 Outlook

Prior to that capital raise, the REIT reported 3Q25 financials on October 27, 2025, posting distributable income of $0.29 per share on total revenue (primarily net interest income) of $7.1 million versus $0.36 per share on total revenue of $8.9 million, representing declines of 19% and 20% (respectively), reflecting the aforementioned lower NIM undercurrents.

Management stated that it planned to close on three properties before the end of the year – on which it made good – providing a 4Q25 distributable income outlook of $0.30 per share based on a range midpoint, covering its newish quarterly distribution of $0.28 per share. The essentially in-line quarter elicited little initial reaction in shares of SEVN, but they did retreat 18% over the subsequent five weeks, bottoming out at a five-year intraday low of $8.30 on December 5, 2025. Somewhat buoyed by the secondary offering, they have rallied slightly off their nadir.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Before raising net proceeds of ~$62 million in December and originating mortgages of $101.3 million in November, the REIT held cash of $77.5 million against debt of $429.4 million with access to another $309.6 million of capacity on its financing facilities on September 30, 2025. Owing to the effects of depreciation and a near 100% payout ratio over the prior five quarters, Seven Hills’ Adj. book value has dropped 2% over the past 12 months to $18.33 per share. As stated previously, its quarterly distribution translates to a current yield of 12.2%.

The small REIT enjoys some small support from the Street (see below). Sell-side analysts are expecting FY26 distributable earnings of $1.21 per share on revenue of $29.4 million in and $1.14 a share on $33.8 million of revenue in FY2026.

Returning to the secondary offering, it was backstopped by Seven Hills’ manager, Tremont Realty Capital, who was compelled to purchase 2.02 million shares, raising its ownership interest to 22.5%.

Verdict:

It is difficult to get enthused about a REIT that has five office properties rated a 4 – representing 23% of its portfolio prior to the November 2025 originations – when delinquency rates on commercial mortgage-backed securities for that asset class are nearing 12%, representing a level higher than the financial crisis of 2008. And to a certain degree, that is why Tremont had to onboard more than 25% of the offering. That said, as a floating rate originator with floors on the preponderance of its mortgages that should prevent NIM compression from additional Fed easing, it has appeal trading at over a 50% discount to Adj. book value with a 12.2% yield. However, the REIT only needs one of its properties to become impaired for another shoe to drop (i.e., its share price). With no liquidity in its options to generate additional ‘yield’ through a covered call trade, I am going to remain to the sidelines as perceptions will likely get worse before a bottoming in CRE.

data.title: Collegium Pharmaceutical: Buying The Projected Growth For 2026
data.description:
My last Collegium Pharmaceutical (COLL) article was back in late 2022, where I discussed how the company was gaining momentum and had plenty of upside following their BDSI acquisition. At that time, Xtampza ER was the primary breadwinner with cash flow improving, and debt was coming down. Since then, COLL is up nearly 150% and is trading close to $50 per share as the company continues to improve profitability and their outlook for 2026 and beyond. The company’s new 2026 guidance strengthens that view, with continued revenue growth, profitability, and a healthy balance sheet. As a result, I remain bullish on Collegium, so I will be on the lookout to add to my position in 2026.

I will review the company’s recent earnings history and what the company expects for 2026. In addition, I discuss the company’s growth drivers, as well as some risks that investors should consider when managing their COLL position. Finally, I take a look at the charts to see if I can identify to an opportunity to manage my COLL position in the near-term.

Earnings History

I believe Collegium’s earnings history over the past few years illustrates a transition from a speculative specialty pharma into a differentiated pharma. Looking at the company’s earnings history below, we can see that in 2020–2021, Collegium was posting inconsistent revenue and weak profitability numbers. However, by late 2022, Collegium’s revenues started to pop and have steadily climbed from roughly $120M per quarter to over $200M per quarter in the second half of 2025, while EPS went from about $1 to $2.25.

I must highlight that their growth has been reliable, with most of their earnings reports revealing strong year-over-year revenue with limited misses on estimates.

So, the market has responded positively as the narrative switched from an opioid-exposed pharma to a dependable specialty-pharma looking to further expand their offerings.

2026 Guidance Points to Growth

Moving to 2026, Collegium is projecting another year of year-over-year growth for their net product revenue. The company is guiding for $805M to $825M, which isn’t a huge bump, but we have to consider that the full-year 2025 is slated to be a record $775M-$785M in net product revenue, which would be roughly 24% growth over 2024. So, I’ll take any growth in 2026 after that large of a bump from 2024 to 2025.

They also expect Jornay PM to bring home $190M-$200M in net revenue, which would make it their primary growth driver for this year. In addition, adjusted EBITDA is projected to be $455M-$475M, with EBITDA margins possibly coming in above 55% at the midpoint.

The steady shift of Collegium’s revenue mix toward higher-growth Journay PM while maintaining the stability of their pain portfolio provides me with some confidence that they will hit their guidance. I understand the company’s guidance wouldn’t be considered explosive growth, but it is still record numbers for the company, and should convert into substantial free cash flow.

Jornay PM Taking the Baton

When I first started covering Collegium, Xtampza ER was the company’s biggest growth opportunity as it looked to claim some of the remnants of the opioid market. Admittedly, that market was going through a lot of reform and litigation, but I believed Xtampza could thrive with its extended release and abuse-deterrent technology. However, the national opioid dispensing rate has dropped from 46.8 per 100 patients in 2019 to 37.5 per 100 in 2023. Although Xtampza ER could still find ways to grow, I think investors should accept that the trends for the prescription opioid market in the United States don't support Xtampza ER.

Thankfully, Xtampza ER can hand the growth baton to Jornay PM, which is now the centerpiece of Collegium’s growth narrative. Unlike traditional ADHD stimulants that are taken in the morning, Jornay PM is evening dosing, thanks to delayed-release and extended-release characteristics that allow it to work upon waking. So, it could benefit patients who show experience with morning ADHD symptoms, which we should expect to be a large proportion of the ADHD population, with roughly 55%-75% of treated children still experiencing symptoms in the morning.

So, Journay PM’s commercial trajectory is not reliant on label expansions to drive growth or even sustain uptake. Instead, Journay PM’s growth will come from deeper penetration into the ADHD market as patients and providers are educated about the product’s differentiation and its benefits for the working population and students who struggle in the morning. Therefore, the big hurdle is refining a targeted sales strategy and executing. If successful, Collegium could see Journay PM hit analyst peak sales estimates of $300M-$400M.

Pain Portfolio Provides Stability

While Jornay PM is going to be the company’s big growth driver, Collegium’s pain portfolio still is a cash generator. As I mentioned above, this portfolio has performed despite opioid market pressures, but the company’s pain products are projected to continue to supply reliable revenue thanks to the company’s commercial execution and abuse-deterrent formulations providing a moat. Looking at the figure below, we can see the company has highlighted the pain portfolio’s recent performance, as well as some of the elements that support its growth outlook.

A surprising growth lever comes from the authorized generic agreement with Hikma Pharmaceuticals, which will supply authorized generic versions of Nucynta and Nucynta ER from Collegium.

Typically, investors fear the day one of their branded products goes generic; however, in this case, the agreement gives Collegium a significant share of net profits from these AG products. Obviously, any generic hurts the company’s branded product; however, getting a cut of the generic’s revenues dampens the impact versus your characteristic generic erosion. In fact, one could frame this as creating a new revenue stream from an existing asset without the commercialization expenses. Indeed, eventually other generics will enter the market, and that stream will start to thin. I still see this as a smart product lifecycle decision to maximize the value of the franchise with stable cash flow without exerting an extra effort to keep it on life-support.

The launch of the authorized generic Nucynta ER is expected to happen in the first quarter of this year, with Nucynta’s launch to follow later this year.

Flexing the Financials

One of the more important updates is that Collegium has refinanced some of their debt. The company recently closed a $980M syndicated credit facility, which helped fully retire their term loan. The new facility has a lower interest rate, which will lead to annualized interest savings.

However, the big upside is that the structure of the facility gives Collegium some additional flexibility thanks to a $300M delayed draw term loan and a $100M revolving credit facility. These options are undrawn at this time; therefore, Collegium has ample liquidity to use for development, additional share repurchasing, or possibly more acquisitions. The company has benefitted from some great acquisitions, so I expect that they will be looking to keep working that playbook to remain on a growth trajectory.

Considering the company’s cash generation, loans, and current bankroll, Collegium could pursue all three simultaneously.

Risks to the Thesis

Despite my long-term bullish outlook on Collegium, there are several risks investors should consider when managing their position. First, Collegium has to execute their commercial strategy on a high level. Jornay PM has strong momentum, but it will be a challenge for the company to sustain these high growth rates, and some of the pieces are being held by payers, patients, and providers to improve adoption. The ADHD market is crowded, so any slowdown in script growth could force investors to start questioning the company’s ability to improve the topline.

Second, investors need to keep an eye out for any generic competition attempting to infiltrate the company’s market. Generics are a persistent threat in the pain management market, and Collegium will eventually have to deal with encroachment. While the company’s Nucynta franchise authorized generic strategy with Hikma helps mitigate the immediate erosion, there should be third-party generic entry in the future, which thin the profit-sharing revenue over time and pressures the margins of the deal. Plus, we can’t expect the company to get similar deals for Belbuca and Xtampza ER.

Third, I would point out that regulatory risks are in-built for a company working in both pain management and neuropsychiatry markets. Perhaps one of the more unnoticed concerns is the restrictions on controlled substances. The DEA quota allocation for methylphenidate API could constrain Jornay's manufacturing capacity. While I don’t think the company has experienced a supply issue, the ADHD market has faced shortages that impact manufacturing. Again, the company has not publicized any issues with getting their API, but it is a new risk to consider now that they have added Jornay to the portfolio.

My final risk to highlight is that the company has to keep maintaining their win-streak by finding synergistic acquisitions. The addition of Jornay PM does open the door to possibly adding more psychotropic drugs; however, finding a deal could be a big challenge. Obviously, not bringing in new assets at an attractive valuation will ultimately impact earnings and could force the company to rely on Short-Term deals to maintain operations.

Despite these risks, I’m upgrading my COLL conviction level from 3 out of 5 to 4 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bioreactor” growth portfolio.

My Plan

COLL is in my “Bioreactor” growth portfolio, so I see the investment as a cash-generating compounder, and not a pure short-term trading vessel. My bullishness on COLL is backed by the company’s profitability thanks to their knack for consistently turning their revenue into EBITDA and free cash flow, yet the ticker is still trading at an attractive valuation for their current performance.

However, my decision to upgrade my conviction has been fueled by the company’s willingness to post bullish guidance for 2026 after a record 2025. In addition, I have to applaud management’s pragmatic efforts to reduce debt, commit to buybacks, and remain disciplined with their M&A efforts.

However, I’m not chasing the stock’s momentum and will hold off on simply adding automatically on pullbacks. I will stick to my system of trading levels in order to avoid following the market noise.

At the moment, COLL is trading above my Sell Level 2 of $39.89, and has been trading above my Sell Level 1 of $25.49 since April of last year. So, I’m deciding to update my trading levels to accommodate COLL’s new technical conditions and get back to building a position, rather than taking profits. My updated Buy Threshold (the highest share price I’m willing to pay for COLL based on fundamental and technical analysis) is $30.33 per share, and Sell Level 1 is $39.89. So, COLL is trading above my Buy Threshold and is even trading above my Sell Level 1, which means I’m not looking to click the buy button at this time. However, I will point out that COLL’s monthly trading range is about $7-$8 per share, and over $10 per share per quarter, so I expect the volatility to provide me an opportunity to add at some point in the coming months. However, I might have to make another adjustment in the second half of the year if the company outperforms their own expectations and COLL remains above my new Buy Threshold.

Overall, my goal is to continue trading my COLL position for the foreseeable future in order to book profits, while still growing a core position with the remnant “House Money” shares to hold for a long-term investment.

data.title: XLSR: High Turnover, High Expense Ratio, And Mixed Returns
data.description:
Today, I would like to initiate coverage of the State Street® US Sector Rotation ETF (XLSR), an actively managed exchange-traded fund with an AUM of close to $884 million.

Before we delve into all the details I usually discuss in my ETF analysis articles, my brief take on XLSR is that its premise (that is, active rotation between sectors driven by "quantitative and qualitative analysis") makes perfect sense, yet the returns it has been delivering since inception in April 2019 are only healthy but far from outstanding.

The fact is, it was incapable of either demonstrating a higher annualized return than the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), which tracks its benchmark, or eking out marginally higher risk-adjusted returns.

At the same time, this vehicle has a 0.70% expense ratio, and I believe most of my dear readers would concur this is quite a burdensome fee, especially compared to IVV's 0.03%.

All of this to say, as I do not see any meaningful arguments to construct a bullish thesis, XLSR deserves a Hold rating.

XLSR Key Facts

  • Inception: April 2, 2019
  • Investment Manager: SSGA Funds Management, Inc.
  • AUM: $883.99 million, as of January 20
  • Expense Ratio: 0.70%
  • Turnover: 183%
  • 5-Year Annualized Total Return (January 2020–December 2025): 12.27% vs. IVV's 15.06%

XLSR Strategy

As we know from the ETF's website, XLSR is managed actively, with the goal being "to provide capital appreciation by tactically allocating among the GICS-defined sectors of the S&P 500 Index."

In the summary prospectus, it is explained that the lynchpin of the strategy is the "proprietary sector selection model." This model processes

...macroeconomic, financial and market data to arrive at a projected return forecast for each Equity Sector.

With these forecasts ready,

...the model then allocates the weightings of each Equity Sector to construct a target Equity Sector allocation (the “Target Equity Sector Allocation”) that seeks to maximize expected return.

However, there is a nuance here:

The Adviser may not fully implement the results of the sector selection model if it believes the model does not take into account all relevant data, or that a different evaluation or weighting of the data is more appropriate.

It is also clarified that some sectors might be ignored at times. For context, just five GICS sectors out of eleven are represented in the XLSR portfolio as of January 20.

As to the holdings,

The Adviser obtains exposure to specific Equity Sectors by allocating assets primarily among the corresponding Equity Sector ETFs and/or Equity Sector Common Stocks based on a proprietary optimization-based portfolio construction process designed to limit the variance between (i) the return of the Fund’s investments and (ii) the return of the Target Equity Sector Allocation.

At this point, we do see not only ETFs but also a few stocks in the XLSR portfolio, which I will discuss in greater depth shortly.

It is also explained that

The Adviser typically rebalances the Fund’s portfolio on a monthly basis, although rebalancing may occur more frequently depending on market conditions.

So its 183% turnover does not look surprising at all.

XLSR Performance: Questionable Track Record, No Consistent Alpha

First and foremost, benchmarked against the S&P 500 index, XLSR has substantially underperformed the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF since its inception in April 2019.

Delving deeper, we see that even though it had some success in 2019 (May–December), 2020, and 2022, the magnitude of its underperformance in other calendar years (especially in 2021) was so large that its annualized return over May 2019–December 2025 was 2.39% lower than IVV's.

Period Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
May–December 2019 0.84%
2020 1.76%
2021 -8.29%
2022 2.46%
2023 -7.38%
2024 -7.34%
2025 -0.50%

Calculated by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

And to give a bit more color on that, I should mention that XLSR's median monthly active return over the 80 calendar months it has in the books was -0.25%. So, this is clearly not an inspiring result.

Next, it is worth looking at how its performance compares to those of peers like the Main Sector Rotation ETF (SECT) and the Anfield U.S. Equity Sector Rotation ETF (AESR). I would like to discuss SECT and XLSR first and then add AESR, as the latter has a much shorter trading history (it was incepted on December 16, 2019).

So since April 2019, SECT has also been unable to outmaneuver IVV, but its total return was much stronger than XLSR's.

Looking at the CAGR and a few selected risk metrics for May 2019–December 2025, we see that XLSR not only had a weaker CAGR than IVV and SECT but also lower risk-adjusted returns (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios).

And since December 2019, XLSR has also underperformed AESR.

So over the January 2020–December 2025 period, XLSR's CAGR was again the weakest in the group.

However, the picture here is a bit more complicated, as AESR still had a higher standard deviation, a deeper maximum drawdown, and a higher downside capture ratio.

XLSR vs. IVV: Portfolio Analysis

As of January 20, we see 20 holdings in the XLSR portfolio, with the bulk of the net assets allocated to five exchange-traded funds (66.48% weight):

  • the State Street® Technology Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLK)
  • the State Street® Health Care Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLV)
  • the State Street® Financial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLF)
  • the State Street® Communication Services Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLC)
  • the State Street® Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLP)

XLK was the top holding with a 23.32% weight.

13 stocks were in second place, with 33.5% weight, with the key names in this group being Alphabet Inc., represented by both Class A (GOOGL) and Class C (GOOG) stock (7.03% and 5.5% weight, respectively). Other asset classes were the U.S. dollar and a money market fund.

So how is XLSR different from IVV in terms of composition, sector, and factor exposures? To answer these questions, I have amalgamated the above-mentioned ETFs' holdings as well as the common stocks XLSR holds to create an aggregate portfolio. So in this basket, we see 265 equities, with Alphabet being the key company, assuming GOOGL has an approximately 7.86% weight and GOOG has 6.17%. For context, Alphabet has a 5.8% weight in IVV (represented by both GOOGL and GOOG). NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) was in second place, with an approximately 8.47% weight (vs. 7.44% in the S&P 500 ETF).

I suppose for the sake of clarity, I should elaborate on how exactly I have arrived at these figures. So for example, in the case of NVIDIA, I have multiplied 0.144744 (NVDA has about 14.47% weight in XLK) by 0.23318215 (as XLK has around 23.3% weight in XLSR) and then added 5.098905% (NVDA's weight in XLSR as the ETF does hold the common stock).

XLSR Sector Mix

As I said above, XLSR has exposure to only five GICS sectors, with the principal one being IT.

Sectors like consumer discretionary, energy, industrials, materials, real estate, and utilities are ignored.

XLSR Factor Mix

Regarding factors, my point here is that the XLSR portfolio looks superior to that of IVV on every front, especially when it comes to GARP characteristics. And these differences are in part driven by the fact that although all equities in the XLSR portfolio (of course, including exposure via the ETFs) are S&P 500 constituents, they have just about 71.6% weight in IVV, as per my calculations.

More specifically, compared to IVV, we see the XLSR portfolio having more comfortable weighted average adjusted earnings yield, Price/Sales, and EV/EBITDA, while also boasting higher weighted average forward EPS, revenue, and EBITDA growth rates. In terms of quality, it is also meaningfully ahead, as illustrated by the WA capital efficiency indicators and its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher.

As to the GARP metrics, XLSR has lower PEG (the weighted average P/E divided by the WA forward EPS growth rate), Price/Sales-to-Growth, and EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios than those of IVV, which is yet another advantage.

Parameter XLSR IVV
PEG 1.039 1.237
Price/Sales-to-Growth 0.531 0.557
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth 0.993 1.074

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Final Thoughts

While I like XLSR's factor story, especially its quality characteristics and the GARP tilt, I cannot construct a bullish thesis using the factor mix only for two reasons.

  • First, this is an active ETF with a monthly rebalancing (there are nuances though) and high turnover, which means the mix might change.
  • Second, for a bullish thesis, I need two components: a convincing factor mix and strong returns (i.e., the ability to outmaneuver the market consistently). We do not have the latter here. XLSR has been delivering mixed results, with three calendar years of consistent underperformance in a row. Risk-adjusted returns are not appealing either.

So in sum, while XLSR has quite an interesting sector rotation approach, I gravitate toward the neutral stance on the name; hence, the Hold rating.

data.title: NPCT: Still Paying Out More Than It Earns
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Nuveen Core Plus Impact Fund (NPCT), I issued a sell rating due to the fund's poor performance and elevated risk profile. Since my prior coverage, the interest rate environment has shifted, and the portfolio has seen some shifts, so I wanted to revisit the fund's value proposition now that we've crossed into the new year. Additionally, the fund has released updated reporting that I wanted to assess to determine NPCT's internal earnings and vulnerabilities.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NPCT's share price has declined by about 4%. The fund has struggled to maintain any positive growth momentum over the last few months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 9.5% over the same time frame. NPCT now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 11.6% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. However, the latest earnings reveal that the fund continues to struggle generating earnings that can fully support the distributions being paid.

Additionally, the fund's internal structure reveals some flaws that limit the appeal during the current market cycle. For instance, NPCT utilizes a large amount of leverage and has exposure to poorly rated securities, which has increased its risk profile and sensitivity to interest rates. The higher cost of debt can negatively impact the fund's ability to regain any positive growth momentum and suppress earnings potential in the short term. So let's start by looking at the fund strategy that the fund implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, NPCT has total managed assets of $503.2M that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing assets. The fund primarily invests in fixed-income investments while putting a specific focus on ESG, including environmental, social, and governance criteria. The fund also provides global exposure to securities that are based outside of the U.S. The majority of the fund is still allocated to U.S. securities (approximately 60%) but provides exposure to regions across Europe, Asia, and Africa as well. Additionally, the fund provides a blended exposure to different income-producing securities, including the following:

  • Investment Grade Corporates: 29.3%
  • Preferred: 19.6%
  • Emerging Market Debt: 18.6%
  • Commercial Mortgage-Backed: 15.0%
  • High-Yield Corporates: 10.4%
  • Short-Term Investments, Other: 2.4%
  • Municipal Bonds: 1.8%
  • U.S. Treasuries: 1.2%
  • Asset-Backed: 1.0%
  • Senior Loans: 0.8%

Expanding on this diversity, the fund also provides exposure to a wide range of sectors to mitigate any sort of concentration risk. As we can see below, NPCT is most heavily allocated to utilities at 29.7% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to banks and financial services securities, which accounts for 14% and 8% of assets, respectively. However, investors must remember that NPCT maintains this exposure through different income securities, rather than traditional equities. Therefore, NPCT's daily price movement may not be directly linked to the movement of each of these sectors.

Valuation & Performance Risk

At the time of my last coverage, NPCT traded at a small discount to NAV of 0.8%. Following the decline in share price, NPCT now trades at a much larger discount to NAV of 8.86%. For reference, NPCT traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.65% over the last five-year period. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that NPCT trades at a deeper discount to NAV compared to peers but still trades above its prior price-to-NAV range only a few years ago.

Since my last coverage, NPCT also released an updated semi-annual report for 2025. This semi-annual report is the latest available data for the fund's operating metrics. Net investment income amounted to $0.22 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.19 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of the period, NPCT's net investment income is aligned to be on par with its prior years. However, the fund's net investment income hasn't historically been sufficient to support distributions on its own. This results in the fund being a bit dependent on net realized gains to bridge that gap in earnings.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on the underlying market conditions. This is why the fund has such a wide range of net realized gains and losses over the last few years. For instance, the fund experienced a massive net realized loss of $6.83 back in 2022 when market indices declined. Similarly, NPCT's net realized gains of $0.19 per share in this semi-annual period are a bit mild considering that market indices continued to rise to all-time highs over the last few quarters. I would love to see net investment income levels substantially improve going forward so that the fund's reliance on net realized gains is reduced. This would improve the NAV growth over time and reduce NAV erosion during market declines. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $18.30 per share
  • 2022: $10.36 per share
  • 2023: $10.08 per share
  • 2024: $10.51 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $11.21 per share

Despite the fund's diverse portfolio, we can see how the failure to generate consistent earnings translates to poor performance over time. Additionally, the higher dividend yield offered doesn't necessarily translate to higher total returns over time. For instance, we can see that NPCT's share price has declined more than 49.4% since its inception. Even when including all distributions paid out, the total return sits at a loss of about 16.8% over the same time frame.

However, one of the biggest issues with NPCT is the heavy use of leverage implemented in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 36.03% of total assets, which is equivalent to approximately $181.3M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment, and it can significantly increase the risk profile of the fund. Since the cost of debt is elevated, this can shrink the fund's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities. The fund's leverage currently has an annualized cost of 4.9%.

The use of leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated. If we also consider the fact that a portion of NPCT's portfolio is allocated to securities with poor credit ratings, this leverage can amplify any negative effects of defaults. For instance, the fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • U.S. Treasury/Agency: 2.4%
  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 50.6%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 34.7%
  • Not Rated: 11.4%
  • Short-Term Investments: 0.8%

The combination of these factors has led to NPCT displaying a large sensitivity to interest rates. Despite the Fed initiating three separate 25 basis point cuts to interest rates in 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Although lower interest rates will likely serve as a catalyst, as they would improve operating spreads, we don't know how long it will take for rates to trend closer to their pre-pandemic levels. Therefore, NPCT's share price is likely to remain suppressed until the interest rate environment significantly improves.

Questionable Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.099 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.6%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.188 per share. Referring to the same semi-annual report, we can see that NPCT has struggled to generate earnings that outpace the distributions paid out to shareholders. As long as the fund continues to pay out more than it earns, the underlying NAV is more likely to erode over time. For reference, here are the annual earnings over the last few years:

  • 2021: $0.38 per share
  • 2022: ($6.16) per share
  • 2023: $0.71 per share
  • 2024: $0.98 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.41 per share

Looking back on these earnings, we can see that NPCT has actually never been able to generate earnings that exceed the distributions being paid. It's hard to gauge what management's strategy is here and why the distributions remain so generous. I believe that NPCT would benefit from a distribution cut because it would reduce the burden of the distribution and also slow the rate of NAV erosion every year. As we can see, the distributions were already reduced twice since the start of 2025, and I believe that we may see further cuts in the near term.

Therefore, it's hard to recommend a position in NPCT at this time, especially for investors seeking a reliable source of income generation. Additionally, the latest reporting indicates that the fund is essentially paying out all of its earnings and bridging the gap in coverage with return of capital distributions. Return of capital distributions directly reduces the NAV with every use, which only exacerbates the NAV erosion over time.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my sell rating on NPCT at this time. The fund continues to pay out more in dividends than it actually earns, which means that NAV erosion is likely to continue in the near term. Additionally, the fund utilizes a large amount of leverage while having exposure to securities with poor credit ratings. Therefore, the fund's growth potential is limited as the cost of debt remains high, since this negatively impacts the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities. I believe that these flaws ultimately lead to the need for the fund to reduce its payouts to help preserve its NAV and stop the share price from eroding further.

data.title: SandRidge Energy: Getting Overvalued For A Long-Term $70 Oil And $3.75 Natural Gas Environment
data.description:
SandRidge Energy's (SD) share price has gone up close to 20% since I last looked at it in early November 2025, going from around the middle of my estimated valuation range to around 9% above the high end of my estimated valuation range. While natural gas prices have rebounded recently, 2026 strip prices remain slightly lower now compared to early November 2025.

SandRidge now is valued (net of cash) at slightly over 1.0x estimated PDP PV-10 at my long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, so I am very close to downgrading it from neutral based on valuation concerns. It only has a modest amount of remaining Cherokee inventory, so its value is mostly in its PDP reserves plus its cash.

PDP Reserve Values

SandRidge reported having PDP reserves with a PV-10 of $305 million at the end of 2024. This was based on $75.48 WTI oil and $2.13 Henry Hub natural gas, though.

Since SandRidge's PDP reserves are natural gas weighted (14% oil, 32% NGLs, and 54% natural gas at the end of 2024), natural gas has more of an impact on its reserve value than oil. The $2.13 Henry Hub natural gas price used in the 2024 reserve calculations was also quite low compared to longer-term expectations.

Thus, I'd estimate that SandRidge's year-end 2025 PDP reserves would have a PV-10 of approximately $450 million based on my long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

This includes the effect of SandRidge's 2025 development program, which should be roughly neutral to slightly positive in terms of its impact on Sandridge's PDP reserve values.

Limited Inventory

SandRidge's oil and gas assets have relatively modest value outside of the PDP assets. It mentioned having 22 two-mile lateral Cherokee locations before, but drilled eight Cherokee wells in 2025 and completed six of them. Thus it may have approximately two years of remaining drilling inventory there.

SandRidge more recently mentioned that its working interest is between approximately 40% to 75% for its Cherokee wells, so there may be under 10 net undrilled Cherokee locations.

SandRidge has also mentioned that it requires consistent $80+ WTI oil and $4+ Henry Hub natural gas to make NW Stack development attractive again, and my long-term commodity prices are below that level.

Notes On Valuation

I had previously estimated that SandRidge would end 2025 with approximately $100 million in cash on hand.

As noted above, I estimate SandRidge's year-end 2025 PDP PV-10 at $450 million at my long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Adjusting that for the current 2026 strip (and then using $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after 2026) may change this to approximately $440 million.

At $15.32 per share, SandRidge has a market cap of around $563 million. Thus, it is essentially currently valued at cash on hand plus slightly over 1.0x PDP PV-10 (even allowing for a bit of value for its Cherokee inventory).

I am maintaining my estimate of SandRidge's value at $11.75 per share to $14.00 per share. The upper end of that range is approximately 0.9x PDP PV-10. Near-term natural gas prices have bounced back well recently due to winter weather expectations, but the 2026 strip remains slightly lower than when I last looked at SandRidge.

SandRidge does have large amounts of owned Midcon infrastructure and approximately $1.6 billion in net operating losses. However, the main value in those items is to allow SandRidge to extract full value out of its PDP assets by keeping its operating cost structure low and allowing it to avoid cash income taxes. Any additional value beyond that remains a hypothetical.

Conclusion

SandRidge's share price has done well recently, but I believe it is getting a bit overvalued now. At 1.0x PDP PV-10 plus net cash plus Cherokee inventory, it would be worth approximately $15 per share. It doesn't have inventory outside of Cherokee that it would develop without significantly higher commodity prices.

I typically have valued SandRidge at approximately 0.7x to 0.9x PDP PV-10, leading to my $11.75 to $14.00 per share valuation range. Thus, for SandRidge to have upside here, the commodity price outlook would need to be better than my long-term values of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I am maintaining a neutral rating on SandRidge right now, but would downgrade it if its share price goes up anymore without significant news or a fundamental change in the longer-term commodity price outlook.

data.title: StoneCo Enters 2026 Cheap, With Credit Set To Drive The Next Leg Of Earnings
data.description:
Even with a strong operational execution over the last two quarters, shares in StoneCo (STNE) have fallen by around 12.7% since I last covered the company in late August 2025, in this case recommending a bullish position, which actually played out in the very short term.

At the time, my original bull case for StoneCo hinged precisely on the management team's excellent execution based on operating leverage in payments and credit, within a macro normalization scenario—especially with the interest rate cycle in Brazil likely to converge to the downside—that would ultimately unlock margin expansion, multiple re-rates, and upside for STNE, even with Brazil having a high structural cost of capital.

However, my bullish call has not materialized, at least in 2H25, due to some specific reasons, such as: (1) change in cut timing of rates, with a central bank in Brazil hinting more strongly at a “higher for longer” environment; (2) TPV slowing down; (3) competition and elasticity post-repricing; and (4) governance overhang, with a recent CEO change.

Looking ahead to 2026, I believe that staying constructive on StoneCo remains the best way to approach the thesis. To begin with, I see the bullish case heading for a clear earnings mix shift this year, along with a maturing credit book, stable mid-teens cost of risk, and a growing deposit funding base that turns what temporary spread gains into more durable profitability—even if TPV growth remains moderate. At current valuations, the ADR is priced for Brazil's high cost of capital. I believe the sum of incremental execution, especially in credit, and any sign of easing in the rate environment could create more asymmetric upside through higher pre-tax earnings and selective multiple expansion.

The Brazilian Macro Setup Entering 2026

In order to talk about a company whose operations take place in Brazil, it's essential to give a macro context to the thesis, which is the background to StoneCo.

I would start by saying that Brazil entered 2026 with a combination that is “good and bad” for a small-business fintech.

On the one hand, Brazil's scenario is one of decelerating inflation, with the IPCA (the index that measures inflation) at 4.26% over the last twelve months.

This figure is fairly “civilized” by Brazilian standards, but it's still not necessarily the kind of perfect convergence that unleashes a cycle of aggressive cuts without caveats.

After all, Brazil's Central Bank (which acts independently, to be clear) has an inflation target of 3% with a 1.5% tolerance. Theoretically, inflation of 4.26% would be within this safety margin and would sustain further cuts throughout 2026.

However, the Selic (Brazil's interest rate) is at an enormous 15% (yes, there is a real interest rate of 10.7%), which is an extremely restrictive level. Even with inflation apparently under relative control, Brazil's Central Bank has reinforced the idea of maintaining interest rates for a “fairly prolonged period”.

In other words, the market may even discuss cuts in 2026, but “higher for longer” still dominates the country's cost of capital.

Without going too deeply into a discussion about Brazil's fiscal situation, which is complex, interest rates in the country tend to be structurally high because when the market starts to see fiscal risk (debt trajectory, uncertain primary result), especially in governments that tend to pour social benefits while the country has low productivity, there is a higher premium on the long yield curve, and this ends up “contaminating” the country's cost of capital.

And with that, the Central Bank needs to keep interest rates high to compensate for the fiscal risk via the exchange rate and expectations.

Okay, that said, now on to the Brazilian consumer and its economic activity. The data still shows a certain resilience even with very high interest rates, but already with signs of losing traction. Retail grew 1% QoQ in November last year and 1.3% YoY, according to the latest data, suggesting that it still has volume, but far from a boom.

The labor market in Brazil is still very strong, with an unemployment rate at 5.2% in the quarter ending November last year. In StoneCo's case, TPV and delinquency in aggregate are supported by this employment data.

However, as mentioned above, the Brazilian tax system remains a multiple structural constraint for the Brazilian equities thesis in general. The debate in 2026, especially as it's a year that will see presidential and legislative elections in Brazil, continues to revolve around deficit/debt and the credibility of a fiscal framework—and this should keep the risk premium high.

Now, one might ask how this all connects with STNE's thesis. I'd start arguing that, (1) for payments, the Brazilian macro imports via volume (TPV) and mix-in which case more PIX/debit equals a lower take rate; (2) for credit, the macro imports twice as much, after all, funding (Selic) and more defaults directly affect credit; and (3) for multiples, the macro ends up acting as a “cap”, even if the company's execution turns out to be good, the fact that it operates in the Brazilian economy ends up defining the WACC of the thesis.

Less TPV, More Credit

Taking a step back and briefly revisiting the latest figures that StoneCo reported, with regard to 3Q25, being quite objective, the company reinforced an “on track” execution, but with two very relevant changes: (1) TPV slowing down; and (2) credit becoming the main driver for 2026.

Based on the most important indicators for the thesis, in 3Q25 STNE posted a consolidated ROE of 24% (up 8 p.p. YoY) and a Financial Services ROE of 33%, which shows that it is printing a mature company ROE. Part of this gain in profitability and quality was due to repricing and the use of deposits as funding, an exchange of “floating revenue” for lower financial expenses.

Moving on, the core engine (MSMB) saw its base grow strongly, with active customers reaching 4.7 million, up 17% YoY, and with 38% of these being heavy users (using more than three Stone solutions). The caveat here is that volume has entered a slowdown phase. MSMB TPV came in at 11% YoY (totaling BRL 126 billion), decelerating ~90 bps YoY, with the highlight being PIX QR growing 49% (more than credit cards, which grew 6% YoY).

The management team recognized in the earnings call weaker same-store sales in smaller customers, and industry growth expectations for 2026 falling to high single digits from low double digits. So, here in this case, the reading is still good numbers, but with growth vectors losing inclination and with marginal deceleration in 3Q25 clear in the mix, and explicit in the forward-looking guidance.

Now, with regard to credit, this is precisely where I see the market pricing 2026 more strongly. StoneCo reported in 3Q25 a loan portfolio with BRL 2.3 billion (up 27% QoQ) and with NPL above 90 days at 5.03%, showing a maturing of the book. Cost of risk fell to 16.8% versus 20.2% in 2Q25, and StoneCo reiterated mid-teens as the expected range.

The management team also made it clear that 2026 tends to have a higher credit contribution in the P&L, simply because the upfront provision effect weighs less as the book matures.

Apart from that, there is a sensitivity to interest rates that also helps explain STNE's macro beta, with the company quantifying that every 100 bps drop in average interest rates would be equivalent to a generation of BRL 200-250 million in EBT, before price pass-through. This is actually a huge number, considering that StoneCo reported EBT of BRL 757.4 million in 3Q25, and helps to understand why STNE trades as a kind of “macro-sensitive compounder”.

Finally, buybacks remain an important part of the thesis, with the company returning BRL 2.8 billion in the last twelve months (~10% distribution yield), where in this case, ~74% of excess capital has already been returned.

In short, I would say that the recent figures posted by StoneCo show a very good execution despite the macro. The company continues to grow its base, increase engagement, and accelerate credit with discipline. The (not so desired) news is that payments (TPV) enter a slower phase, and then 2026 becomes more and more about credit, efficiency, and operational leverage, and not so much about multiples of TPV, in my reading.

Leadership Transition and the 4Q25 Setup

The latest news about StoneCo involves the CEO transition, as Mateus Scherer (former CFO) will take over as CEO from March this year. Current CEO Pedro Zinner is moving to the board (tending towards chairman), and there are other changes at the C-Level of the company, such as a new COO. The ADR's initial reaction was to fall, typical of uncertainty about a transition.

Regarding these events, I would make the following points. Firstly, the internal succession tends to bring a greater perception of risk reduction, considering that the CFO who already dominated the narrative of capital allocation, funding, and guidance. Secondly, there is a real risk with the departure of the strategy/marketing leader, who may suggest a change in cultural focus, which is very relevant in a competitive market such as acquiring.

But what will really tell whether the C-level transition will be a risk or a non-event will be the results to be delivered over the next two to three quarters, as the market is looking to see: (1) TPV/market share stability, continuity of the deposit playbook, and credit underwriting.

Ahead of StoneCo’s 4Q25 results, due to be reported on March 18th, based on the management team's comments, one can anticipate: (1) TPV in this quarter is seasonally strong, but YoY growth slightly lower (probably high single digits at ~10%); (2) TPV mix worsening (temporarily) yield, with CFO hinting 4Q with more PIX and debt, pressuring gross profit yield, even with higher volumes; (3) credit could be strong, with a reasonable expectation of further strong sequential portfolio growth, higher P&L contribution and stable cost-of-risk (mid-teens); (4) strong EPS ($0.47 representing 17% YoY), but with seasonality noise, with buybacks helping to support EPS.

Valuation, Risks, and Final Remarks

StoneCo enters 2026 trading at just 8.4x forward earnings, and at a PEG of 0.5—multiples seemingly de-risked and reflecting the high risk embedded in operating in Brazil's economy. Nevertheless, the company remains an SMB compounder in the country, with high ROE and consistent execution.

However, the marginal short-term price driver for STNE tends to be “macro vs. cost of capital”, and not necessarily “execution vs. consensus”. In practice, this means that what could unlock the rise in STNE would be: (1) interest rate cuts in Brazil gaining traction, even if gradual—directly impacting EBT, and expansion of multiples; (2) credit contributing more to P&L, with the cost of risk stabilizing in the mid-teens; (3) evidence that TPV decelerating is a macro issue, and not a structural loss of share to competitors; and (4) maintenance of buybacks as a technical floor for the thesis.

On the contrary, the risks of this materializing are summarized in: (1) a scenario with a higher Selic rate for longer and fiscal stress, raising the WACC and compressing multiples; (2) competition and take rate where the PIX grows (worsening yield, although improving retention), and players such as PagSeguro (PAGS), Cielo, Rede (Itaú Unibanco (ITUB)) and MercadoPago (MercadoLibre (MELI)) testing price aggressiveness; (3) credit with a fast-growing book and NPL>90 days at 5%, where if the macro worsens, the market penalizes early.
